surface.name: Box Free Developer Plan default CCG app and Get Current User
surface.selection_basis: Box's current Free Developer Plan documents immediate signup activation and automatically creates and authorizes a default Client Credentials Grant application. The authentication documentation shows how CCG obtains an access token, and the Get Current User reference provides a concrete minimal authenticated call with a 200 user response.

alternatives_considered.name: Platform App with developer token and Get Current User
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: The token guide demonstrates `/users/me`, but the first-application guide does not select that endpoint as its terminal test.

alternatives_considered.name: Postman List items in folder quickstart
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: This has an explicit first-call summary but requires the separate Postman collection and environment setup.

alternatives_considered.name: Box CLI
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: The first-application guide presents CLI as an alternative test interface.

entry_point.developer_intent: Create a first Box Platform application and make a first authenticated API call without a preselected authentication method or tool.
assumed_prior_state: The developer has arrived after discovery.
The developer does not yet have a Box Free Developer account or an API access token.

documented_first_success.normalized_outcome: A newly activated Box Free Developer account uses its automatically created and authorized CCG application to obtain an access token and retrieve the authenticated service-account user.
documented_first_success.observable_completion_signal: `GET https://api.box.com/2.0/users/me` returns HTTP 200 and a user JSON object with fields such as `id`, `type`, `name`, and `login`.

boundary_evidence.official_label_or_terminal_state: The Free Developer Plan supplies the default authorized CCG app, the CCG guide supplies the token flow, and the Get current user reference demonstrates the authenticated call and 200 response.

documented_first_success.why_this_is_the_boundary: This is the shortest complete current official path from no account to a successful authenticated Box API response, using the app and authorization state Box creates by default.

prerequisites.requirement: Create a Box Free Developer account with name, email, password, terms acceptance, and Get Started; the account activates immediately.
Viewing the CCG client secret requires Box two-factor authentication. The access token identifies the default app's service account and must be protected.

primary_path.action: Open the Box developer signup page, enter name and email, create a password, accept the terms, and select Get Started.
details: The Free Developer account activates immediately.
Box automatically creates a default Client Credentials Grant application and authorizes it for the free developer account.
primary_path.input: Name, email address, password, and terms acceptance.
primary_path.output: Active Free Developer account, Developer Console access, and default authorized CCG app.
primary_path.success_signal: The Developer Console is available immediately and shows the default app.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: No activation wait or separate admin approval is documented for the default free-account app.

primary_path.action: Open the default CCG app's Configuration and collect the client ID, client secret, and enterprise ID needed to authenticate as its service account.
details: Two-factor authentication must be enabled to view and copy the client secret.
The client secret is confidential and must not be placed in client-side code, email, public forums, or repositories.
primary_path.input: Default authorized CCG app and Box 2FA.
primary_path.output: Client ID, client secret, and enterprise subject ID.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Without 2FA the client secret cannot be viewed; incorrect app credentials later produce `invalid_grant`.

primary_path.action: POST the CCG credentials to `https://api.box.com/oauth2/token` with `grant_type=client_credentials`, `box_subject_type=enterprise`, and the enterprise ID as `box_subject_id`.
details: The default service-account route uses the enterprise subject type.
Server authentication returns an access token without a refresh token.
primary_path.input: Client ID, client secret, enterprise ID, and CCG grant fields.
primary_path.output: Bearer access token and expiry metadata.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Mismatched credentials or an invalid subject produce `invalid_grant`; request a new token after the access token expires.

details: The endpoint retrieves the user represented by the access token.
For the default server-authenticated app, this is the app's service account.
primary_path.output: HTTP response containing the current user.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: An absent or invalid token produces an authentication error; app scopes and represented-user permissions govern other endpoints.

primary_path.action: Inspect the response JSON and confirm it identifies the authenticated Box service-account user.
details: The documented 200 response includes user identity and account fields.
primary_path.success_signal: A user object with fields such as `id`, `type`, `name`, and `login` is present.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: A non-200 response means the first authenticated call has not succeeded.

branches.condition: The developer creates another server-authenticated app instead of using the default app.
branches.path: In Developer Console select New App, choose Server, enter the app name, select Client Credentials Grant or JWT as the server authentication method, and select Create. Configure scopes and application access before authorization.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Manual app creation replaces the auto-created app and can add a separate authorization gate.

branches.condition: The app is created inside a Business or Enterprise account rather than the Free Developer account.
branches.path: If the creator is an Admin or Co-Admin, select Authorize in the app Configuration. Otherwise select Submit, wait for the Admin or Co-Admin decision email, and continue only after approval.
branches.effect_on_first_success: The free account's automatic app authorization is replaced by enterprise administrator approval and an unbounded wait.

branches.condition: OAuth 2.0 is selected instead of the default CCG route.
branches.path: Create an OAuth 2.0 Platform App, configure a redirect URI, build the authorization URL, redirect the user to Box, obtain user grant, receive the authorization code, and exchange it for access and refresh tokens before calling `/users/me`.
branches.effect_on_first_success: End-user authorization and redirect handling replace server-account token acquisition.

branches.condition: JWT is selected instead of CCG.
branches.path: Create a server JWT app, enable 2FA, generate or supply a key pair, obtain enterprise authorization where required, sign a JWT assertion, exchange it for a token, and then call an allowed endpoint.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Key-pair generation, signed assertions, and administrator authorization replace the client-secret-only CCG route.

branches.condition: Developer token plus direct curl is selected.
branches.path: Name and create the Platform App, review General Settings, configure required scopes, generate the developer token in Configuration, copy it before its 60-minute expiry, send `GET https://api.box.com/2.0/users/me` with `Authorization: Bearer`, and inspect the authenticated-user response. A missing or invalid token returns 401; scope, access, or user restrictions can still block calls.
branches.effect_on_first_success: The first API result identifies the Box user represented by the token.

branches.path: Complete the separate Box Postman collection and environment setup, select the Box environment, open Folders > List items in folder with `folder_id` 0, select Send, and inspect the response Body. If authentication fails, refresh the expired token and retry.
branches.effect_on_first_success: The response lists items in the authenticated user's root folder and the page summarizes that the first Box API call was made.

branches.path: Verify that client ID and client secret belong to the same app, confirm that the chosen subject ID is allowed by the app access level, and retry the token request.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Credential or subject configuration must be repaired before the first API call.

friction_gates.description: A Free Developer account requires name, email, password, terms acceptance, and Get Started, but activates immediately without billing.
CCG requires client ID, protected client secret, enterprise ID, and Box 2FA to reveal the secret.
The token endpoint must return an active access token; server-auth tokens do not include a refresh token and must be requested again after expiry.
Scopes, application access, enterprise authorization, endpoint restrictions, and token-user content permissions all affect success.
The first call is complete only when Get Current User returns HTTP 200 and a user object.

sources.title: Free Developer Plan
sections_used: Sign up as a Box Developer
evidence_supported: Name, email, password, terms, and Get Started signup
Automatically created and authorized default CCG app

sources.title: Access Tokens
evidence_supported: No refresh token for JWT or CCG
OAuth access and refresh token distinction

uncertainties.question: Does the automatically created free-account CCG app expose its enterprise ID in the same Configuration section as the client credentials for every account?
uncertainties.impact: If the identifier appears elsewhere, the developer must navigate to another app or account settings page before requesting the token.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The Free Developer Plan confirms automatic app creation and authorization, while the CCG guide names the required enterprise ID without documenting the free-account screen layout.

excluded_after_success: Additional content endpoints and application features after the authenticated identity call.
Production OAuth lifecycle, app enablement, App Center publication, webhooks, enterprise rollout, metadata, AI, uploads, and custom portals are excluded.
